text:
A short educational article about photosynthesis explains how plants convert light, water, and carbon dioxide into stored chemical energy. The main idea is simple: leaves contain chlorophyll, chlorophyll absorbs sunlight, and the plant uses that energy to build sugars.

This example is written in a calm textbook style. It has a clear topic, a few related facts, and no navigation or advertising text. A chunker should probably keep the paragraphs together if the target size allows it, because they form one complete explanation.

# Introduction
Urban water systems move clean water into homes and carry wastewater away for treatment. A city usually needs reservoirs, pumps, pipes, sensors, repair crews, and public rules for conservation.

## Storage
Storage tanks help balance demand during the day. Morning use may rise quickly, while nighttime use is lower. Engineers plan capacity so that pressure remains stable even when many households use water at the same time.

## Maintenance
Pipes age, valves fail, and small leaks become expensive when nobody notices them. Regular inspection and simple reporting tools can prevent a minor repair from becoming a district-wide service interruption.

## Summary
A reliable system is not only a set of machines. It is also a schedule, a budget, a safety practice, and a way for residents to understand what is happening.

This article describes how a community garden can organize shared tools, watering schedules, and seasonal planting days. Volunteers often choose a coordinator, keep a simple calendar, and agree on rules for compost, seeds, and harvest sharing.

Top stories: climate briefing, local school board update, weekend events, market snapshot, travel delays, editor picks, video highlights, podcasts, archive.

The actual page content is a brief report about a library extending its evening hours during exam week. Students asked for a quiet place after regular classes, and staff created a temporary schedule with extra volunteers.

Related links: campus map, library rules, room booking, print services, accessibility, feedback form, mobile app, social media.

We want to estimate the slope of a curve near a point. For a function f(x), the derivative can be introduced as a limit: $$ f'(a) = \lim_{h \to 0} \frac{f(a+h)-f(a)}{h}. $$ This expression compares the change in output to the change in input.

Example: if f(x)=x^2, then \frac{(a+h)^2-a^2}{h}=\frac{2ah+h^2}{h}=2a+h. As h approaches 0, the expression approaches 2a. The formula is compact, but the surrounding explanation matters for chunking because equations alone are not enough context.

This bilingual note explains a classroom exercise about summarizing technical text. Students first read a short paragraph in English, then write a brief explanation in Russian.

Цель задания простая: проверить, сохраняется ли основной смысл после пересказа. Если текст содержит термины вроде tokenization, context window или dataset filtering, студент должен объяснить их своими словами, а не просто скопировать фразу.

A chunker should preserve both languages in one coherent unit when the topic does not change.

Question: I have a small dataset of web pages and I want to split each page into chunks before running a model. Should I split by sentence, paragraph, or fixed token windows?

Answer: Start with paragraph-based splitting because it often preserves meaning better than a blind fixed window. Then enforce a maximum token count so that very long paragraphs do not break the model context.

Comment: Also watch for repeated footers and cookie banners. They can dominate the sample if every page includes the same boilerplate.

Introducing the Northline Desk Lamp, a compact LED lamp for reading, sketching, and late-night work. It has three brightness levels, a matte aluminum arm, and a weighted base designed for small desks.

Features: warm and cool light modes, USB-C power, low heat output, replaceable shade, and a simple touch control. Limited launch offer: buy two and save 15 percent. Free shipping on orders over $50.

Customer note: This product description is artificial and included only to test commercial language, repeated feature lists, and promotional calls to action.

# Overview
A classroom sensor network can help students understand temperature, humidity, and light as measurable signals rather than abstract numbers. The lesson begins with a simple question: why do two corners of the same room sometimes feel different? Students collect readings, compare them, and discuss possible causes.

## Instruments
The activity uses low-cost sensors, a shared spreadsheet, and a wall clock. Each team records the same type of measurement at the same time so the results can be compared later. The point is not to build a perfect laboratory system, but to show how repeated observations become evidence.

## Data collection
During the first round, students place sensors near a window, near a door, beside a radiator, and in the center of the room. They write down the reading, the location, and a short note about nearby conditions. If a window is open or sunlight hits the desk, that note matters.

## Interpreting variation
Small differences are expected. A sensor near direct sunlight may report a higher temperature than a sensor in shade. A sensor near the door may change when people enter or leave. Students should learn that variation is not automatically an error; sometimes it is the signal they are trying to understand.

## Limits of the method
The readings are useful, but they are not complete. Cheap sensors may drift, students may copy a number incorrectly, and the room may change during the exercise. The class should discuss uncertainty instead of pretending that every value is exact.

## Conclusion
The final report asks each team to describe one pattern and one possible explanation. A good answer connects the measurement to the physical setting, mentions at least one limitation, and avoids claiming more certainty than the data supports.

This web-like article describes how a small neighborhood repair workshop organizes public sessions for bicycles, lamps, and simple household items. The organizers publish a schedule, ask visitors to bring missing parts when possible, and keep a table for tools that should not leave the room.

The first section of the page tells a practical story. On a rainy Saturday morning, volunteers open the doors, label the workbenches, and sort requests into categories. A loose brake cable goes to the bicycle table, a flickering desk lamp goes to the electrical table, and a torn backpack waits for the sewing corner.

Some paragraphs are useful, while others are page furniture. Subscribe to updates, follow us on social media, manage cookie preferences, and review our community guidelines. These notices are not harmful by themselves, but they can become noisy if the same wording appears on every page in a dataset.

The workshop also keeps simple safety rules. Visitors should not solder without supervision, children should stay near an adult, and damaged batteries should be placed in a separate container. The rules are written in plain language because the event is meant for beginners.

A later paragraph explains why the workshop matters. Repairing an object can teach mechanical reasoning, reduce waste, and make people more confident about maintenance. Even when an item cannot be fixed, the owner may learn what failed and how to choose a better replacement.
